sourceTitle: A project management system for your design team that connects all your work

questions.question: What is the main benefit of consolidating all design work, feedback, and assets inside Notion?

options.text: It eliminates the need for a separate design tool like Figma.
options.explanation: Notion can embed Figma files, but it doesn’t replace a dedicated design tool.

options.text: It reduces tool‑juggling and gives the team a single source of truth for each project.
options.explanation: Centralizing everything in Notion means designers spend less time switching apps and can find all relevant info in one place.

options.text: It automatically generates design assets for the team.
options.explanation: Notion is a workspace, not an asset‑generation engine.

options.text: It guarantees faster load times for large design files.
options.explanation: Load speed depends on the file host; Notion simply embeds the file.

questions.question: Which slash command creates a full‑page board that can be used to track design initiatives?

options.text: /table
options.explanation: /table creates a table view, not a board.

options.text: /board
options.explanation: Typing /board and selecting “Board – Full page” creates the board used in the guide.

options.text: /calendar
options.explanation: /calendar creates a calendar view, which is different from a board.

options.text: /list
options.explanation: /list creates a list view, not a board.

questions.question: Which property type should you use to assign the lead designer or project owner to a card?

options.text: Select
options.explanation: Select lets you choose from predefined options, but it doesn’t link to a Notion user.

options.text: Person
options.explanation: The Person property lets you tag a specific Notion user, perfect for assigning a designer.

options.text: Date
options.explanation: Date records a calendar date, not a person.

options.text: Checkbox
options.explanation: Checkbox is a binary field, not suitable for assigning a person.

questions.question: How can you change the columns of a board so that projects are grouped by priority instead of status?

options.text: Drag the priority property onto the board and drop it.
options.explanation: Properties can be shown on cards, but they don’t directly change column grouping.

options.text: Click the "Group by" button in the upper‑right and select the Priority property.
options.explanation: The "Group by" control re‑creates the board columns based on any Select, Multi‑select, or Person property, such as Priority.

options.text: Create a new view named "Priority" and set its type to Calendar.
options.explanation: Changing the view type to Calendar won’t affect board column grouping.

options.text: Rename the existing status columns to the priority values.
options.explanation: Renaming columns only changes the label; it doesn’t automatically group cards by a different property.

questions.question: You need a view that shows only the mobile design projects. Which series of actions will achieve this?

options.text: Create a List view, add a filter where the "Type" property equals "Mobile".
options.explanation: A List view with a filter on the Type (Select) property set to Mobile displays exactly those projects.

options.text: Create a Board view and rename the "In progress" column to "Mobile".
options.explanation: Renaming a column does not filter the data; it only changes the column label.

options.text: Add a Sort rule that orders cards by the "Device" property.
options.explanation: Sorting changes order but does not filter out non‑mobile items.

options.text: Use the "Group by" button and select the "Device" property.
options.explanation: Grouping creates separate columns for each device type but still shows all items.

questions.question: What is the purpose of adding a Select property called "Feedback" with a tag "RFC" (Request for Comment) to a project page?

options.text: To automatically send an email to the entire team.
options.explanation: Select properties don’t trigger emails on their own.

options.text: To signal to teammates that the page is open for comments and feedback.
options.explanation: Tagging a page with "RFC" makes it clear that the design is seeking input from others.

options.text: To lock the page so only the owner can edit it.
options.explanation: Locking is a separate permission feature, not a Select tag.

options.text: To change the color of the page header.
options.explanation: Select tags affect metadata, not visual styling of the header.

questions.question: A designer wants a personal view that only shows the tasks assigned to them. Which workflow creates this view?

options.text: Create a new page, type /link, select "Create linked database", choose the team board, then add a Filter where "Person" is the current user.
options.explanation: Linking the board and applying a filter on the Person property creates a personal view that syncs with the main board.

options.text: Duplicate the entire board and manually delete all cards that aren’t theirs.
options.explanation: Duplication creates a static copy; it won’t stay in sync with updates.

options.text: Change the board’s "Group by" setting to the designer’s name.
options.explanation: Grouping changes column layout but does not filter out other users’ tasks.

options.text: Add a Checkbox property called "My Tasks" and check it on every card they own.
options.explanation: While possible, it requires manual upkeep and isn’t the recommended quick filter method.

questions.question: Which scenario best illustrates the use of a Relation property between a design board and an engineering board?

options.text: Linking each design project to the corresponding engineering tickets so that updates in one board appear on the other.
options.explanation: A Relation creates a two‑way link between items in separate databases, allowing designers and engineers to see each other's related tasks.

options.text: Adding a Select property named "Status" to both boards.
options.explanation: Select properties are independent; they don’t create cross‑database connections.

options.text: Embedding a Figma prototype inside a design page.
options.explanation: Embedding is about media, not about linking separate Notion databases.

options.text: Creating a shared template for both design and engineering teams.
options.explanation: Templates standardize page structure but don’t establish relational links between items.

questions.question: Why would you toggle certain properties to be visible on the cards in a board view?

options.text: To reduce the amount of data stored in the database.
options.explanation: Visibility settings affect only the UI, not the underlying data.

options.text: To give a quick, high‑level overview of key information without opening each card.
options.explanation: Showing properties on cards lets team members scan status, owner, or dates at a glance, improving efficiency.

options.text: To permanently lock those properties so they can’t be edited.
options.explanation: Visibility does not affect edit permissions.

options.text: To automatically sort the board by those properties.
options.explanation: Sorting is a separate function; visibility only changes what’s displayed.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a recommended step when building a Project Kickoff template?

options.text: Add an H2 heading called "Overview" with sub‑headings for "Problem Statement" and "Proposed Work".
options.explanation: This structure is explicitly suggested in the guide.

options.text: Include a Select property named "Type" to categorize the project.
options.explanation: Using a Select property for type is a best‑practice mentioned for templates.

options.text: Insert a random image unrelated to the project to make the page look lively.
options.explanation: The guide does not advise adding unrelated images; templates should stay focused on project context.

options.text: Add colored prompt text under headings to guide contributors on what information to fill in.
options.explanation: Using colored prompts is recommended to give clear guidance.
